Q.2. Solve-

I am a digit number. I have a zero in the ones place and a 5 in the tens place.

Answer  $ \underline{50} $
before lunch, Sarah had 2 apples. During lunch, she ate 3 more apples. After lunch, she had apples in total.
1. How many apples did Sarah have before lunch?  $ \underline{2} $
2. How many apples did Sarah eat during lunch?  $ \underline{3} $
3. How many apples did Sarah have after lunch?
4. How many apples did Sarah have in total?  $ \underline{\text{2 + 3 5 = 10}} $

parsing_res_list.block_content: Grade: 1Subject: MathsTopic: High Order Cognitive Abilities
You are planning a surprise birthday party for the child of your househelp, and you have to design everything from the very beginning.

You start by writing down how many friends you want to invite. Each time the number grows big, you bundle ten friends into one special "team" and see how many full teams and how many single friends you have left. Then, you give each friend a fun number name.
Then you plan how to decorate the room by choosing your own balloon pattern—maybe grouping them in 2s, 5s, or 10s—You can draw it in your notebook too.
You also choose how big the birthday cake should be and think about whether the number of slices will be enough for all the friends you invited.
Next, you pack the return gifts. You arrange the gifts in straight rows of ten and see if any gifts are left over at the end.
Finally, you organise the cups, plates, and chocolates. Instead of counting them one by one, you stack them in tens and then place the extra ones beside them, just like a part supply shopkeeper.
Are you ready to plan a wonderful surprise party for your friend?
Share with us how you felt organising a party for your househelp child.

markdown.text:
Grade: 1Subject: MathsTopic: High Order Cognitive Abilities
Date ___ ##### High Order Cognitive Abilities ##### Abilities Empathy Problem Solving Decision Making You are planning a surprise birthday party for the child of your househelp, and you have to design everything from the very beginning. You start by writing down how many friends you want to invite. Each time the number grows big, you bundle ten friends into one special "team" and see how many full teams and how many single friends you have left. Then, you give each friend a fun number name. Then you plan how to decorate the room by choosing your own balloon pattern—maybe grouping them in 2s, 5s, or 10s—You can draw it in your notebook too. You also choose how big the birthday cake should be and think about whether the number of slices will be enough for all the friends you invited. Next, you pack the return gifts. You arrange the gifts in straight rows of ten and see if any gifts are left over at the end. Finally, you organise the cups, plates, and chocolates. Instead of counting them one by one, you stack them in tens and then place the extra ones beside them, just like a part supply shopkeeper. Are you ready to plan a wonderful surprise party for your friend? Share with us how you felt organising a party for your househelp child.

Grade: 1Subject: MathsTopic: Real life application / Tell me Why?

parsing_res_list.block_content: In a lift, if floor numbers are not in order, how will you know whether the lift is going up or down?
Imagine a chocolate broken into pieces and sharing it with your friends. You may give away a few pieces to your friend, but you know... It was one whole chocolate.
When you add chairs for guests, sweets for friends, candles on a cake - silently at the back of your mind you are doing addition.

Without comparison, will it be possible for you to know that the World's talles statue of Lord Ram was unveiled at Gokarn Partagali Math in the year 2025? Would you be able to know who is the fastest runner, the smallest animal?
From helping your mother in the kitchen to sharing snacks with your friends, life full of stories. And these stories turn into memories that stay with you for lifetime.
Ask your child to count chairs, plates or sweets when guests come so they see how addition happens in real life.
When you read news or watch TV together point cut dates, ages, distances are score's so your child notices numbers every where.

markdown.text:
Date ___ Imagine a World without Numbers! In a lift, if floor numbers are not in order, how will you know whether the lift is going up or down? Imagine a chocolate broken into pieces and sharing it with your friends. You may give away a few pieces to your friend, but you know... It was one whole chocolate. When you add chairs for guests, sweets for friends, candles on a cake - silently at the back of your mind you are doing addition. Without comparison, will it be possible for you to know that the World's talles statue of Lord Ram was unveiled at Gokarn Partagali Math in the year 2025? Would you be able to know who is the fastest runner, the smallest animal?
From helping your mother in the kitchen to sharing snacks with your friends, life full of stories. And these stories turn into memories that stay with you for lifetime. Ask your child to count chairs, plates or sweets when guests come so they see how addition happens in real life. When you read news or watch TV together point cut dates, ages, distances are score's so your child notices numbers every where.

parsing_res_list.block_content: Grade: 1Subject: MathsTopic: Concept Introduction
Comparison is a process or method to determine whether a number or quantity is smaller, greater, or equal to another number or quantity according to their values or amount.
E.g. The symbols used for comparing numbers or quantities are “☐”, which means “greater than”; “☐”, which means “less than”; and “=”, which means “equal to”.
Ascending Order the process of arranging the numbers in increasing order, i.e., from smallest value to largest value. The lowest value is written first and the highest value is written at the last.
Descending Order the process of arranging the numbers in decreasing order, i.e., from largest value to smallest value. The highest value is written first and the lowest value is written at the last.
Number Bonds is a pair (combination of two numbers) of numbers that add up to give the sum as a specific number.
Addition is the process of combining two or more numbers or quantities to find their total sum. The operation for Addition is “+”
Story Problem serves as a narrative tool that presents a real-world situation requiring a mathematical solution.

markdown.text:
Grade: 1Subject: MathsTopic: Concept Introduction
Date ___ Comparison is a process or method to determine whether a number or quantity is smaller, greater, or equal to another number or quantity according to their values or amount. E.g. The symbols used for comparing numbers or quantities are “☐”, which means “greater than”; “☐”, which means “less than”; and “=”, which means “equal to”. Ascending Order the process of arranging the numbers in increasing order, i.e., from smallest value to largest value. The lowest value is written first and the highest value is written at the last. The word Ascending means “Going Up” Descending Order the process of arranging the numbers in decreasing order, i.e., from largest value to smallest value. The highest value is written first and the lowest value is written at the last. The word Descending means “Going Down” Number Bonds is a pair (combination of two numbers) of numbers that add up to give the sum as a specific number. E.g. Number bonds of 5 are 4+1, 2+3, 5+0. Addition is the process of combining two or more numbers or quantities to find their total sum. The operation for Addition is “+” Story Problem serves as a narrative tool that presents a real-world situation requiring a mathematical solution.

##### Fill in the blanks. (Use the word from the bracket)
1. When the numbers are arranged from the _____ to the _____ they are in their ascending order. (smallest, greatest)
2. In the ascending order the_____number comes first. (smallest, greatest)
3. In the ascending order the_____number comes last. (smallest, greatest)
4. In the ascending order of 34, 70, 90, ___ will come first. (70, 90,
5. In the descending order ___ number comes first. (great smallest)
6. In the descending order of 36, 83, 22_____ will come first. (83, 22,
8. The second smallest two-digit number in descending order is ___ (10, 11, 19)

Subject: MathsTopic: Number Sense-2
Grade: 1

Q1. Tom planted 52 seeds, Harry planted 13 seeds, Sam planted 94 seeds and Shyam planted 40 seeds on the environment day in their school. Arrange the numbers in ascending and descending order to find out who planted the maximum and least number of seeds.

Q2. Four friends Sanya, Tanya, Maria and Tina went to a beach to play. They saw that the beach was very dirty so they thought of cleaning it. Sanya picked up 3 bags of cans, Tanya picked up 2 bags of plastic bottles, Maria picked up 7 bags of plastic waste and Tina picked up 5 bags of waste paper. The beach looked clean. Then they put up sign boards "Kindly do not litter on the beach". Arrange the numbers in ascending and descending order to find out who picked up the maximum and least number of litter bags.
_____ picked up maximum number of litter bags.
Descending order(DO)-___ picked up least number of litter bags.
